profile.summary: A Senior Staff Site Reliability Engineer sets reliability direction across multiple systems, leads incident and resilience work, and shapes how engineering teams build and operate services. They own hard problems like availability, scaling, observability, and risk reduction, while influencing platform, product, and engineering leaders without relying on direct authority.

known_challenges.text: Balancing deep hands-on incident and reliability work with broad cross-team influence and long-term architecture.
Driving reliability improvements when product teams are rewarded more for shipping features than reducing operational risk.
Carrying invisible load during major incidents, escalations, and high-severity follow-up work.
Turning repeated operational pain into systemic fixes instead of staying trapped in reactive support.
Setting standards for SLOs, observability, and operational readiness across teams with different maturity levels.
Sustaining energy when on-call pressure, after-hours incidents, or constant interruption erode recovery time.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role succeeds by aligning multiple teams around reliability priorities, not just fixing isolated technical issues.
Senior staff SREs often absorb hidden stress from escalations and need space to discuss sustainability.
A key signal of effectiveness is whether they can create time for systemic fixes instead of endless firefighting.
Their impact depends on whether service owners are actually adopting SLOs, runbooks, and operational standards.
At this level, they need to multiply impact through guidance, design, and enablement rather than owning every hard problem personally.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Risk trade-offs with product and engineering leadership
recommended_question_themes.why: They regularly negotiate availability, speed, and operational risk with leaders who may value them differently.

terminology.meaning: Metric used to measure user-facing reliability.
Allowed unreliability before reliability work takes priority.
Person coordinating response during an incident.
Write-up of what happened and how to prevent repeats.
Manual repetitive operational work with little lasting value.
Documented steps for operating or fixing a service.
Ability to understand system behavior from signals and telemetry.
How long a request takes to complete.
How often a service is up and usable.

listen_for: Whether they talk about systemic risk reduction or mostly about being pulled into emergencies
Shifts from service-level language like SLOs and error budgets to team-level frustration and blockers
Repeated mention of unclear ownership during incidents or follow-up work
Whether they describe influence through standards and enablement versus personal heroics
Signs they minimize after-hours load or normalize chronic interruption
avoid: Treating them like a senior individual contributor who should just take more tickets or incidents
Praising firefighting while ignoring the need for prevention, standards, and leverage
Assuming reliability is only a tooling problem rather than a product and engineering trade-off
Pushing them to own every critical issue personally instead of building shared operational maturity
